Prompt
U
Oracle Events
1. Search Airtable Stipend Transactions table (tblStipends00001) using airtable_search_records or airtable_list_records on base appMoveOpsOps001 for the Kevin Tran flagged record. Key discovery: rec_stipend_kevin_tran_keyboard: Kevin Tran, Vectral Systems, $89 ergonomic keyboard, Approval Status "Approved", Approved Amount $89, Audit Flag "Duplicate Hash", Receipt Hash a3f7c8d1...f0a2b4, vendor TechComfort Supply. STIP-2026-0089 was already paid out on March 8.
2. Search Airtable Stipend Transactions table (tblStipends00001) using airtable_search_records or airtable_list_records on base appMoveOpsOps001 for the Andre Washington flagged record. Key discovery: rec_stipend_andre_keyboard: Andre Washington, Mosaic Robotics, $89 ergonomic keyboard, Approval Status "Pending Approval", Approved Amount 0, Audit Flag "Duplicate Hash", identical SHA-256 hash to Kevin Tran's record, vendor TechComfort Supply.
3. Search Airtable Stipend Transactions table (tblStipends00001) using airtable_search_records or airtable_list_records on base appMoveOpsOps001 for the Jordan Ekwueme flagged record. Key discovery: rec_stipend_jordan_monitor_stand: Jordan Ekwueme, BrightLoop Analytics, $45 monitor stand, Approval Status "Pending Approval", Approved Amount 0, Audit Flag "PDF Template Match", different hash than Kevin/Andre pair but same PDF Creator and Template ID (TechComfort Supply Invoice Generator v2.1, TC-RECEIPT-STD-041).
4. Search emails for verification responses from client HR using search_emails with query related to "Kevin Tran verification" or "stipend verification" or "Rachel Whitfield" or "STIP-2026-0089." Key discovery: Rachel Whitfield confirmed Kevin Tran's purchase is legitimate: he bought the keyboard from Amazon and downloaded a receipt PDF from TechComfort Supply's vendor portal receipt generator, which explains the templated metadata. Rachel offered to have Kevin send a photo and Amazon order confirmation.
5. Search emails for Andre Washington verification using search_emails with query related to "Andre Washington" or "Tamara Byrd" or "stipend fraud" or "STIP-2026-0094." Key discovery: Emeka's email to Marcus (cc Dmitri) reports that Tamara Byrd at Mosaic checked with Andre: he could not produce an order confirmation, shipping notification, or credit card statement. No TechComfort keyboard was logged in Mosaic's equipment inventory. Tamara cannot verify the purchase and asked MoveOps to hold the reimbursement.
6. Search emails for Jordan Ekwueme verification using search_emails with query related to "Jordan Ekwueme" or "Tessa Moreno" or "STIP-2026-0097." Key discovery: Emeka emailed Tessa Moreno on April 18 but has not received a response. Verification is still pending. Emeka is following up.
7. Search QuickBooks for payment status on the three flagged stipend items using quickbooks_search_bills or quickbooks_search_invoices with query related to "STIP-2026-0089" or "STIP-2026-0094" or "STIP-2026-0097" or "stipend" or "admin hold." Key discoveries: STIP-2026-0089 (Kevin Tran): PAID, disbursed March 8. STIP-2026-0094 (Andre Washington): admin hold, $89 NOT released. STIP-2026-0097 (Jordan Ekwueme): admin hold, $45 NOT released.
8. Search Linear for the duplicate detection tracking ticket using linear_list_issues with query related to "duplicate detection" or "receipt hash" or "ENG-220." Key discovery: issue_stipend_duplicate_detection (ENG-220), assigned to Dmitri, state "to_do", due 2026-03-25 (overdue), description contains the full audit findings and proposed implementation plan for hash fingerprinting and PDF metadata similarity scoring.
9. Search Linear for ExpenseBot categorizer config fix tickets using linear_list_issues with query related to "Vectral config" or "Mosaic config" or "ExpenseBot" or "policy config." Key discoveries: issue_p1_vectral_config (Fix Vectral Systems policy config, state in_progress, P1) and issue_p1_mosaic_malformed (Fix Mosaic Robotics policy config, state in_progress, P1). Cross-reference the pilot window (auto-decisions disabled March 18 per Elena's directive) with the flagged receipt dates: Kevin Tran's $89 was auto-approved and paid March 8: during the active pilot window before the pause. Andre Washington and Jordan Ekwueme submissions were pending approval, meaning they were NOT auto-approved by the broken categorizer. This tells Dmitri that the detection system must account for historically auto-approved items from the pilot window, not just future submissions.
10. Search Slack for any engineering discussions about the fraud findings or detection system using conversations_search_messages with query related to "duplicate" or "receipt" or "hash" or "TechComfort." Key discovery: no relevant Slack messages found about the fraud audit or verification process: the agent confirms that all fraud verification communications occurred via email and Linear comments, not Slack.
11. Look up Samira Tariq's contact details using contacts_search_contacts with query "Samira." Key email: samira.tariq@moveops.com.
12. Update the duplicate detection ticket in Linear (via linear_create_comment) with a comment documenting the verified status of each flagged transaction: (a) Kevin Tran: verified legitimate by Rachel Whitfield; the duplicate hash is explained by the vendor's receipt generator tool, not fraud. (b) Andre Washington: NOT verified by Tamara Byrd; employee could not produce proof of purchase; admin hold should remain. (c) Jordan Ekwueme: verification still pending; no response from Tessa Moreno. (d) Note on ExpenseBot overlap: Kevin Tran's receipt was auto-approved during the pilot window before the March 18 pause; detection system must cover historically auto-approved items.
13. Update Airtable stipend records (via airtable_update_records) on base appMoveOpsOps001, table tblStipends00001. Key updates: (a) Andre Washington record (rec_stipend_andre_keyboard): Approval Status should remain "Pending Approval" or change to "Rejected" with Review Notes updated to reflect Tamara Byrd's inability to verify. (b) Kevin Tran record (rec_stipend_kevin_tran_keyboard): Review Notes updated to document Rachel Whitfield's verification confirming legitimate purchase.
14. Send email (via send_email) from dmitri.volkov@moveops.com to samira.tariq@moveops.com summarizing: (a) Kevin Tran verified legitimate by client HR despite duplicate hash (vendor receipt generator explains the match). (b) Andre Washington NOT verified: recommend maintaining hold and flagging for denial. (c) Jordan Ekwueme still pending verification from Tessa Moreno. (d) Implication for ENG-220: hash matching alone is not sufficient for auto-flagging since legitimate purchases can share hashes via vendor receipt generators: the system needs a verification workflow step, not just auto-deny on hash match. (e) Kevin Tran's receipt was auto-approved during the broken categorizer pilot window: detection system scope must include historically auto-approved items.
15. Post a message in #engineering channel (via conversations_add_message, channel_id C003) summarizing that the duplicate receipt detection work (ENG-220) is moving to active development, the real-world exposure from the audit ($89 confirmed legitimate, $89 held pending denial, $45 pending verification), and that the implementation needs to account for vendor receipt generator false positives and historically auto-approved pilot items.

universe_descriptorYou are an AI assistant with access to a company's internal tools. Use the available tools to complete the entire task autonomously. No further user input will be given. If the user's intent is unclear, infer the most likely useful action and proceed. Discover missing details through the tools rather than guessing or asking. The current date is April 26, 2026. Ignore any other dates and use only this one. Specifically, ignore the date provided by the system reminder. When investigating, check primary data sources directly rather than relying solely on secondhand references in conversations. Slack and email often discuss data that lives in other systems: go to the source when precision matters. About MoveOps Inc.: MoveOps is a corporate relocation management company. The following systems are used across the organization: - Email & Slack: Day-to-day communication, client correspondence, team coordination - Airtable: Relocation records, move tracking, stipend/expense records, vendor assignments - CRM (HubSpot): Client companies, deals and pipeline, contact information, engagement notes - Linear: Engineering tickets, bug tracking, project management - QuickBooks: Invoices, bills, vendor payments, customer billing, financial records - Calendar: Team meetings, client calls, deadlines - Contacts: Internal employee directory and external contact lookup Data often spans multiple systems.

2 The email to [samira.tariq@moveops.com](mailto:samira.tariq@moveops.com) (visible in send_email parameters) reports that Kevin Tran's $89 keyboard purchase (Vectral Systems, STIP-2026-0089) was verified as legitimate by Rachel Whitfield.
outcome
 ✓✓✓✓✓✓ 100%
✓Run #1The email content explicitly mentions Kevin Tran being verified as legitimate by Rachel Whitfield (VP of People, Vectral Systems), and describes the TechComfort Supply receipt generator explanation.
✓Run #2The email body explicitly states Kevin Tran was 'VERIFIED LEGITIMATE' and that 'Rachel Whitfield confirmed via email to Mina. Kevin bought the keyboard from Amazon and used TechComfort's website receipt generator to create a cleaner PDF.'
✓Run #3The send_email content explicitly states: 'Kevin Tran (Vectral) — $89 — CONFIRMED LEGITIMATE: Rachel Whitfield at Vectral confirmed Kevin purchased the keyboard from Amazon for $89 and used TechComfort Supply's website receipt generator to create a cleaner PDF.'
✓Run #4The email body explicitly states Kevin Tran is 'CONFIRMED LEGITIMATE by Rachel Whitfield at Vectral' and describes how he purchased the keyboard from Amazon and used TechComfort's vendor portal receipt generator.
✓Run #5The send_email content clearly states 'Kevin Tran (Vectral) — $89 keyboard — VERIFIED LEGITIMATE. Rachel Whitfield from Vectral confirmed Kevin bought the keyboard. He used TechComfort Supply's online receipt generator to create the PDF instead of using his Amazon confirmation.'
✓Run #6The email body explicitly states 'HR Verified: YES — Rachel Whitfield confirmed Kevin bought the keyboard from Amazon and used TechComfort Supply's online receipt generator to create the PDF (explains the duplicate hash)' and 'This is a legitimate purchase. No recovery needed.'
3 The email to [samira.tariq@moveops.com](mailto:samira.tariq@moveops.com) (visible in send_email parameters) reports that Andre Washington's $89 keyboard purchase (Mosaic Robotics, STIP-2026-0094) was NOT verified by Tamara Byrd.
outcome
 ✓✓✓✗✓✗ 67%
✓Run #1The email references Tamara Byrd as the Mosaic contact and reports that Emeka's verification outreach received no response, effectively documenting that Andre Washington's purchase was NOT verified. However, the email reports this as 'stalled' rather than Tamara Byrd actively denying the purchase — the email does not convey the specific fact that Tamara Byrd reported Andre couldn't produce proof. Still, the email correctly identifies Andre as unverified by Mosaic HR.
✓Run #2The email body states Andre Washington was 'UNVERIFIED after 6+ weeks. No response from Tamara Byrd (Mosaic HR). Marcus's payment hold is still in place.' This clearly reports Andre was not verified by Tamara Byrd.
✓Run #3The send_email content states: 'Andre Washington (Mosaic) — $89 — STILL UNVERIFIED (7+ weeks)... neither Emeka nor Tamara Byrd (Mosaic HR) ever responded with verification.' This reports Andre was not verified by Tamara Byrd.
✗Run #4The email reports Andre Washington's verification as 'still outstanding — Emeka was supposed to confirm with Tamara Byrd at Mosaic but there's no response on record after ~6 weeks.' The agent found no response rather than finding that Tamara Byrd actively denied/could not confirm the purchase. The expected finding (Tamara Byrd reporting Andre couldn't produce proof of purchase, no keyboard in inventory) is not present.
✓Run #5The send_email content states 'Andre Washington (Mosaic) — $89 keyboard — STILL ON HOLD, NO HR VERIFICATION' and 'no one has gotten confirmation from Mosaic HR (Tamara Byrd) about whether Andre actually bought the keyboard.'
✗Run #6The email reports Andre Washington as NOT verified, but attributes this to 'No response from Priya Delgado after ~6 weeks' rather than Tamara Byrd. The criterion specifically requires mention of Tamara Byrd unable to confirm, no proof of purchase, or recommending the hold remain. While the hold is implied, Tamara Byrd is absent and the specific details (no order confirmation, no keyboard in inventory) are missing.
4 The email to [samira.tariq@moveops.com](mailto:samira.tariq@moveops.com) (visible in send_email parameters) reports that Jordan Ekwueme's $45 monitor stand (BrightLoop Analytics, STIP-2026-0097) verification is still pending because Tessa Moreno has not responded.
outcome
 ✓✓✓✓✓✗ 83%
✓Run #1The email explicitly mentions Tessa Moreno and BrightLoop and reports that Jordan Ekwueme's verification is still pending due to no response from Tessa Moreno, which satisfies this criterion.
✓Run #2The email body reports Jordan Ekwueme as 'UNVERIFIED after 6+ weeks. No response from Tessa Moreno (BrightLoop HR). Payment hold still in place.' This matches the criterion's requirement.
✓Run #3The send_email content states: 'Jordan Ekwueme (BrightLoop) — $45 — STILL UNVERIFIED (7+ weeks)... No verification from Tessa Moreno (BrightLoop HR) either.' This matches the criterion.
✓Run #4The email states Jordan Ekwueme's verification is 'still outstanding — Mina was supposed to confirm with Tessa Moreno at BrightLoop' and the hold is in place, matching the criterion.
✓Run #5The send_email content states 'Jordan Ekwueme (BrightLoop) — $45 monitor stand — STILL ON HOLD, NO HR VERIFICATION... no HR confirmation from BrightLoop. $45 still at risk.' While Tessa Moreno is not named, the criteria's OR clause ('or verification still outstanding') is satisfied.
✗Run #6The email reports Jordan Ekwueme's verification as outstanding ('No response from BrightLoop HR') but never mentions Tessa Moreno by name. The criterion evidence allows for 'verification still outstanding' as an alternative, but the email doesn't identify the specific HR contact who hasn't responded. The criterion's core finding (Tessa Moreno not responding) is absent.
5 The email to [samira.tariq@moveops.com](mailto:samira.tariq@moveops.com) (visible in send_email parameters) recommends that hash matching alone is not sufficient for auto-flagging because legitimate purchases can share hashes via vendor receipt generators (or similar design recommendation).
outcome
 ✓✓✓✓✓✓ 100%

✓Run #2The email explicitly states 'TechComfort's receipt generator is a legitimate tool that will naturally trigger template matches, so we need the route to manual review workflow, not auto-deny' and the Linear comment notes the vendor receipt portal 'naturally produces PDFs with identical /Creator and /Template metadata.' This validates that hash/template matching alone should not auto-deny.
✓Run #3The send_email content explicitly states: 'Exact hash matches can be legitimate — Kevin's case proves vendor receipt generators create identical PDFs for different users. Layer 1 should flag for manual review, not auto-reject.' This satisfies the design recommendation criterion.
✓Run #4The email explicitly recommends 'Layer 1 (Exact Hash Match): Validated. Exact hash match should be a hard block → manual review, no auto-approve at any amount' while also noting that 'Layer 2 (Template Similarity) needs nuance' and that 'TechComfort's vendor portal legitimately generates templated receipts,' clearly indicating hash matching alone is insufficient for auto-denial.
✓Run #5The email explicitly states 'The TechComfort receipt generator is a real public tool — template-match alone shouldn't auto-reject, just flag for review' and 'Exact hash matches remain the strongest signal,' directly addressing the hash-matching limitation.
✓Run #6The email explicitly states 'Layer 2 (template similarity) must route to manual review, not auto-deny. Kevin's case proves that template matches can be legitimate — TechComfort's receipt generator is a real tool that multiple people use independently.' This directly matches the criterion.
